title: Safe And Responsible AI Use
summary: Use AI with stronger data judgment, verification habits, and policy awareness so speed never outruns trust.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Know Your Data Boundaries
lessons.subtitle: The first safety question is what material belongs in the prompt at all.
lessons.objective: Classify inputs by sensitivity and choose safer ways to work when the material is risky.
lessons.useWhen: The first safety question is what material belongs in the prompt at all.
lessons.whyItMatters: The easiest AI mistake is not a bad answer. It is putting the wrong information into the system in the first place.

coreConcepts.title: Separate Public From Sensitive
coreConcepts.text: Not all work material carries the same risk. Customer data, employee data, contracts, and regulated information need stricter handling.

coreConcepts.title: Minimize Before You Paste
coreConcepts.text: If you can abstract, redact, or summarize the sensitive parts while preserving the task, do that first.

coreConcepts.title: Use Approved Paths
coreConcepts.text: Company-approved tools, internal instances, and redaction workflows matter more than individual convenience.

workedExample.scenario: A writer wants AI help on a customer escalation note that contains account details.
workedExample.approach: They remove identifying information and provide the issue pattern, approved facts, and desired tone instead of raw customer data.
workedExample.result: They still get useful writing help without moving sensitive details into the prompt.
workedExample.verification: They confirm the final note reintroduces only the approved customer-specific facts from the source system.

artifact.label: Checklist
artifact.title: Before you paste work material
items: Is this approved for AI use?
Can I redact or abstract it?
Do I need a safer workflow?

doThisNow.task: Pick one AI use case you do today and define what data must be removed before prompting.
steps: Write a redacted version you could safely use.

commonMistakes: Pasting raw customer or employee data by default.
Assuming paid access equals full approval.
Ignoring redaction because it feels inconvenient.

roleVariants.manager: Frame AI requests around a deliverable, risk level, and reviewer before you ask for output.
roleVariants.writer: Use AI as a drafting and editing partner, then review for voice, facts, and audience fit.
roleVariants.analyst: Treat AI as a hypothesis generator and explainer, not as a trusted source of record.
roleVariants.operator: Anchor every prompt in the real workflow, inputs, and downstream handoff.

sourceNotes: Reviewed for business IC relevance and durable workflow guidance.
Keep verification and organizational policy checks explicit in future revisions.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: What To Never Trust Without Review
lessons.subtitle: Some outputs always deserve a second look.
lessons.objective: Identify categories of AI output that require explicit human review before use.
lessons.useWhen: Some outputs always deserve a second look.
lessons.whyItMatters: Not all AI mistakes are equal. Review effort should scale with the business consequence of being wrong.

coreConcepts.title: High-Consequence Claims
coreConcepts.text: Legal, compliance, financial, customer-facing, and policy statements deserve stricter checks.

coreConcepts.title: Specifics Need Evidence
coreConcepts.text: Dates, names, numbers, pricing, citations, and quoted language should be verified against a real source.

coreConcepts.title: Advice Is Still Drafting
coreConcepts.text: Even when AI suggests a strong course of action, it is preparing a recommendation for human judgment, not making the decision.

workedExample.scenario: A program manager asks AI to summarize new contract language.
workedExample.approach: They treat the output as a drafting aid and compare every obligation statement to the actual contract.
workedExample.result: The summary becomes useful without pretending the AI performed legal review.
workedExample.verification: Every clause used in the summary is checked against the source document.

artifact.label: Review Rule
artifact.title: Mandatory review categories
items: Anything that changes risk or cost

doThisNow.task: Write your personal "always review" list for the work you do most often.
steps: Save the list beside your prompt library.

commonMistakes: Reviewing every output the same way.

roleVariants.manager: Frame AI requests around a deliverable, risk level, and reviewer before you ask for output.
roleVariants.writer: Use AI as a drafting and editing partner, then review for voice, facts, and audience fit.
roleVariants.analyst: Treat AI as a hypothesis generator and explainer, not as a trusted source of record.
roleVariants.operator: Anchor every prompt in the real workflow, inputs, and downstream handoff.

sourceNotes: Reviewed for business IC relevance and durable workflow guidance.
Keep verification and organizational policy checks explicit in future revisions.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Verification As A Habit
lessons.subtitle: Fast review beats late correction.
lessons.objective: Build a lightweight verification sequence that fits daily work instead of feeling like extra bureaucracy.
lessons.whyItMatters: Verification only works if it is simple enough to use under deadline pressure.

coreConcepts.title: Check The Critical Few
coreConcepts.text: Review the parts that could cause harm: claims, commitments, owners, numbers, and policy statements.

coreConcepts.title: Use Source-First Review
coreConcepts.text: Go back to the original note, document, spreadsheet, or policy before trusting the polished output.

coreConcepts.title: Mark Uncertainty
coreConcepts.text: If something cannot be confirmed quickly, label it as open instead of pretending certainty.

workedExample.scenario: An analyst uses AI to prepare a project status summary from meeting notes.
workedExample.approach: They verify dates, owners, and blockers against the source notes and mark uncertain items as open questions.
workedExample.result: The summary remains fast to produce without spreading bad details.
workedExample.verification: The analyst checks three items systematically before sending: owner, deadline, and risk statement.

artifact.label: Sequence
artifact.title: Three-point verification
items: Check anything that could create risk if wrong.

doThisNow.task: Attach a three-point verification routine to one of your common AI workflows.
steps: List the three fields you must check.
Use it on the next output you generate.

commonMistakes: Assuming shorter outputs need less review.
Ignoring uncertainty because the draft looks finished.

roleVariants.manager: Frame AI requests around a deliverable, risk level, and reviewer before you ask for output.
roleVariants.writer: Use AI as a drafting and editing partner, then review for voice, facts, and audience fit.
roleVariants.analyst: Treat AI as a hypothesis generator and explainer, not as a trusted source of record.
roleVariants.operator: Anchor every prompt in the real workflow, inputs, and downstream handoff.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Working Within Policy
lessons.subtitle: Good AI habits include knowing what your organization has already decided.
lessons.objective: Translate organizational AI policy into practical day-to-day behavior.
lessons.useWhen: Good AI habits include knowing what your organization has already decided.
lessons.whyItMatters: Adoption stalls when policy feels abstract. It succeeds when people know exactly what rules mean in the moment of work.

coreConcepts.title: Know The Allowed Tools
coreConcepts.text: Approval often applies to specific tools, plans, connectors, or data classes rather than to AI in general.

coreConcepts.title: Know The Escalation Path
coreConcepts.text: When the work falls into a gray area, knowing who decides is part of responsible use.

coreConcepts.title: Turn Policy Into Prompts
coreConcepts.text: Simple reminders such as "use source text only" or "do not include identifiers" make policy practical.

workedExample.scenario: A team member is unsure whether internal financial notes can be used in an approved AI workspace.
workedExample.approach: They check the policy, confirm the allowed data classes, and ask the reviewer when the case is ambiguous.
workedExample.result: The work continues without guessing at the rule.
workedExample.verification: The final workflow is documented so the next person does not have to rediscover the same boundary.

doThisNow.task: Write a one-page translation of your organization’s AI policy into plain working rules.
steps: List who to ask when unsure.

commonMistakes: Treating approval as universal across all data types.
Relying on hearsay instead of the actual rule.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: A Safe Daily Checklist
lessons.subtitle: Use the same short checklist whenever you work with AI.
lessons.objective: Create a daily checklist that balances speed, privacy, and verification.
lessons.useWhen: Use the same short checklist whenever you work with AI.
lessons.whyItMatters: A short checklist is easier to maintain than a long policy deck, and it keeps good habits visible under pressure.

coreConcepts.title: Check The Inputs
coreConcepts.text: Is this prompt safe for the selected tool?

coreConcepts.title: Check The Output
coreConcepts.text: What claims or commitments need review before use?

coreConcepts.title: Check The Destination
coreConcepts.text: Who will read this and what happens if it is wrong?

workedExample.scenario: A coordinator uses AI several times per day for communication and planning.
workedExample.approach: They keep a visible checklist next to their prompt library and apply it before sharing any final output.
workedExample.result: The workflow stays fast while avoiding careless errors.
workedExample.verification: The checklist includes a final stop on factual and approval-sensitive claims.

doThisNow.task: Draft a five-line AI checklist you can use daily.
steps: Use them on your next AI-generated deliverable.

commonMistakes: Using it only for big tasks.
Skipping it when the output looks polished.

roleVariants.operator: Anchor every prompt in the real workflow, inputs, and downstream handoff.

outcomes: Use AI with better data judgment.
Add lightweight verification to daily work.
nextModuleBridge: Next, move from foundations into daily productivity workflows where AI can save immediate time.
